era_context: The year 1864 fell in the midst of an era of global upheaval and consolidation. The American Civil War entered its decisive phase as Union forces adopted a strategy of total war. In Europe, Prussian ambitions under Bismarck advanced through the Second Schleswig War against Denmark, a critical step toward German unification. China's Taiping Rebellion — the bloodiest civil war in history — reached its conclusion with the fall of Nanjing. European imperialism continued its expansion in Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific. The Industrial Revolution was accelerating global economic integration, while social movements — abolitionism, early labour organising, and nascent Red Cross humanitarianism — were reshaping the political landscape.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (sources filtered almost entirely through European explorers, missionaries, and traders; indigenous perspectives largely unrecorded)
Central Asia (Russian imperial expansion lens dominates)
Pacific Islands (limited systematic records beyond European contact zones)
Indigenous peoples of the Americas and Australia (overwhelmingly filtered through colonial accounts)

events.title: Fall of Nanjing ends the Taiping Rebellion
events.description: On 19 July 1864, Qing imperial forces under Zeng Guofan's Xiang Army breached the walls of Nanjing (Tianjing), the Taiping Heavenly Kingdom's capital, after a prolonged siege. Hong Xiuquan, the Taiping leader who had proclaimed himself the younger brother of Jesus Christ, had died on 1 June 1864, likely from illness, leaving the movement leaderless. The fall of the city was accompanied by massive slaughter — possibly over 100,000 killed — as Qing forces showed little quarter. The capture of Nanjing effectively ended the Taiping Rebellion, a fourteen-year civil war that had devastated much of southern China and killed an estimated 20–30 million people, making it the deadliest conflict of the nineteenth century by a wide margin.
key_figures: Zeng Guofan (Qing commander, Xiang Army organiser)
Zeng Guoquan (Qing general, led the siege)
Hong Xiuquan (Taiping Heavenly King, died June 1864)
Hong Tianguifu (young successor, captured and executed)

sources.name: Zeng Guofan's memorials and correspondence (published posthumously)
Jonathan Spence, God's Chinese Son: The Taiping Heavenly Kingdom of Hong Xiuquan (1996)
Stephen Platt, Autumn in the Heavenly Kingdom: China, the West, and the Epic Story of the Taiping Civil War (2012)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by Qing military records, Western observers present in the region, and surviving Taiping documents. Death toll estimates vary widely but the event itself is beyond dispute.

events.title: Battle of the Wilderness and Overland Campaign begins
events.description: On 5–6 May 1864, Union forces under Ulysses S. Grant engaged Robert E. Lee's Army of Northern Virginia in the dense forests of the Wilderness in Spotsylvania County, Virginia. The battle was tactically inconclusive, with approximately 29,000 combined casualties. Crucially, unlike his predecessors, Grant did not retreat after the bloody encounter but instead moved south toward Spotsylvania Court House, signalling a new Union strategy of relentless offensive pressure. The Overland Campaign that followed — through Spotsylvania (8–21 May), North Anna (23–26 May), and Cold Harbor (31 May – 12 June) — inflicted devastating casualties on both sides but steadily pushed Lee's army toward Richmond and Petersburg.
key_figures: Robert E. Lee (Confederate commander, Army of Northern Virginia)
George G. Meade (Union commander, Army of the Potomac)

sources.name: Official Records of the War of the Rebellion, Series I, Vols. 36–40
Grant, Personal Memoirs of U.S. Grant (1885)
Gordon Rhea, The Battle of the Wilderness, May 5–6, 1864 (1994)

events.certainty_note: Exhaustively documented through military records, correspondence, memoirs, and press coverage from both sides.

events.title: Siege of Petersburg begins
events.description: After the costly Battle of Cold Harbor (1–3 June), Grant executed a daring manoeuvre, crossing the James River and attacking Petersburg, a critical rail junction supplying Richmond. Initial assaults (15–18 June) failed to take the city, and the campaign settled into a protracted siege lasting from mid-June 1864 to April 1865. The siege represented a shift in the war's character toward trench warfare that foreshadowed the Western Front of World War I. Petersburg tied down Lee's army in a war of attrition that the outnumbered and under-supplied Confederacy could not win, slowly strangling its logistical capacity.

sources.name: Official Records of the War of the Rebellion, Series I, Vols. 40–42
Earl Hess, In the Trenches at Petersburg: Field Fortifications and Confederate Defeat (2009)

events.certainty_note: Documented by extensive military records, engineering reports, photographs, and correspondence.

events.title: Sherman's March to the Sea
events.description: After capturing Atlanta on 2 September 1864, Union General William T. Sherman set out on 15 November with approximately 62,000 troops on a march from Atlanta to the port city of Savannah, which he reached on 21 December. Sherman's forces cut a swath of destruction 60 miles wide across Georgia, destroying railroads, factories, farms, and infrastructure in a deliberate strategy of total war aimed at breaking the Confederacy's economic capacity and civilian morale. The march demonstrated that the Confederate government could not protect its own territory and further demoralised the Southern war effort. Sherman presented Savannah to Lincoln as a 'Christmas gift.'
key_figures: William T. Sherman (Union commander, Military Division of the Mississippi)
Joseph Wheeler (Confederate cavalry commander, harassed Sherman's flanks)

sources.name: Sherman, Memoirs of General W.T. Sherman (1875)
Official Records of the War of the Rebellion, Series I, Vol. 44
Joseph Glatthaar, The March to the Sea and Beyond (1985)

events.certainty_note: One of the most documented military campaigns in American history; extensive records, diaries, and post-war accounts survive from both Union soldiers and Southern civilians.

events.title: Fall of Atlanta
events.description: After a prolonged campaign through northern Georgia beginning in May, Sherman's forces captured Atlanta on 2 September 1864 when Confederate General John Bell Hood evacuated the city. Atlanta was a major Confederate rail hub, manufacturing centre, and symbol of Southern resistance. Its fall had enormous military and political consequences: it revived Northern morale at a critical moment when war-weariness had made Lincoln's re-election uncertain, effectively destroying the Democratic peace platform's argument that the war was unwinnable. Sherman subsequently ordered the evacuation of the civilian population before beginning preparations for his March to the Sea.
key_figures: John Bell Hood (Confederate commander, Army of Tennessee)
Joseph E. Johnston (Confederate commander, replaced by Hood in July)

sources.name: Official Records of the War of the Rebellion, Series I, Vols. 38–39
Albert Castel, Decision in the West: The Atlanta Campaign of 1864 (1992)

events.certainty_note: Exhaustively documented by military records, telegrams, press coverage, and memoirs.

events.title: Second Schleswig War — Prussia and Austria defeat Denmark
events.description: The Second Schleswig War (1 February – 30 October 1864) pitted Prussia and Austria against Denmark over the duchies of Schleswig, Holstein, and Lauenburg. The decisive Battle of Dybbøl on 18 April saw Prussian forces storm Danish fortifications, and the Danish island of Als fell in late June. Denmark sued for peace; by the Treaty of Vienna (30 October), Denmark ceded all three duchies to joint Austro-Prussian administration. The war was a pivotal moment in Otto von Bismarck's strategy for Prussian-led German unification, as the resulting Austro-Prussian condominium over the duchies created a pretext for the Austro-Prussian War of 1866. For Denmark, the loss was traumatic, reducing the kingdom by roughly one-third of its territory and population.
key_figures: Prince Friedrich Karl of Prussia (military commander)
General Eduard Vogel von Falckenstein (Prussian commander)

sources.name: Treaty of Vienna, 30 October 1864 (diplomatic archives)
Danish and Prussian military dispatches, 1864
Michael Embree, Bismarck's First War: The Campaign of Schleswig and Jutland 1864 (2006)

events.certainty_note: Thoroughly documented through diplomatic archives, military records, and press coverage in multiple languages.

events.title: First Geneva Convention signed
events.description: On 22 August 1864, representatives of twelve states signed the Geneva Convention for the Amelioration of the Condition of the Wounded in Armies in the Field. The convention established that wounded soldiers should be treated regardless of nationality, that medical personnel and facilities should be considered neutral, and adopted the Red Cross on a white background as a protective emblem. The treaty was the direct result of Henry Dunant's campaigning following his experience at the Battle of Solferino (1859) and the founding of the International Committee of the Red Cross in 1863. It represented the first multilateral treaty of international humanitarian law and laid the foundation for the modern laws of armed conflict.
key_figures: Henry Dunant (founder of the Red Cross movement)
Guillaume-Henri Dufour (Swiss general, chaired the diplomatic conference)

sources.name: Convention for the Amelioration of the Condition of the Wounded in Armies in the Field, Geneva, 22 August 1864 (original treaty text)
Henry Dunant, A Memory of Solferino (1862)
David Forsythe, The Humanitarians: The International Committee of the Red Cross (2005)

events.certainty_note: The original treaty document survives; proceedings of the diplomatic conference are well recorded.

events.title: Battle of Cold Harbor
events.description: On 3 June 1864, Grant ordered a frontal assault against entrenched Confederate positions at Cold Harbor, northeast of Richmond. The attack was repulsed with devastating Union losses — approximately 7,000 casualties in less than an hour — while Confederate losses were minimal. The battle became synonymous with the futility of frontal assaults against entrenched positions and is often cited as Grant's worst mistake of the war. He himself later wrote: 'I have always regretted that the last assault at Cold Harbor was ever made.' The disaster prompted Grant to abandon direct approaches to Richmond and instead cross the James River to target Petersburg.
key_figures: George G. Meade (Union Army of the Potomac commander)

sources.name: Official Records of the War of the Rebellion, Series I, Vol. 36
Grant, Personal Memoirs of U.S. Grant (1885)
Ernest Furgurson, Not War But Murder: Cold Harbor 1864 (2000)

events.certainty_note: Well documented; casualty figures debated in detail but the scale of the disaster is not in dispute.

events.title: Lincoln re-elected President
events.description: On 8 November 1864, Abraham Lincoln won re-election, defeating Democratic candidate George B. McClellan by a wide electoral margin (212–21) though with a closer popular vote (55–45%). The election was conducted despite the ongoing civil war, with Lincoln initially expecting to lose due to war-weariness and heavy casualties during the Overland Campaign. The fall of Atlanta in September transformed the political landscape. Lincoln's victory ensured the continuation of the war to unconditional surrender and made the abolition of slavery via constitutional amendment a certainty. Notably, soldiers in the field were allowed to vote in most states, and they overwhelmingly supported Lincoln.
key_figures: George B. McClellan (Democratic candidate, former Union general)
Andrew Johnson (Lincoln's vice-presidential running mate, replacing Hannibal Hamlin)

sources.name: Election returns, 1864 (state and federal records)
John Waugh, Reelecting Lincoln: The Battle for the 1864 Presidency (1997)
David Long, The Jewel of Liberty: Abraham Lincoln's Re-Election and the End of Slavery (1994)

events.certainty_note: Election results are a matter of public record; the political context is richly documented in correspondence, press, and diaries.

events.title: Battle of Mobile Bay
events.description: On 5 August 1864, a Union naval squadron under Admiral David Farragut attacked the Confederate defenses of Mobile Bay, the last major Gulf port open to Confederate blockade runners. Despite the presence of mines (then called torpedoes) — which sank the ironclad USS Tecumseh — Farragut reportedly ordered 'Damn the torpedoes, full speed ahead!' and led his fleet into the bay. The Confederate ironclad CSS Tennessee was captured after a fierce engagement. The closure of Mobile Bay severely tightened the Union blockade and, like the fall of Atlanta, boosted Northern morale ahead of the November election.
key_figures: Gordon Granger (Union army commander, Fort Gaines and Fort Morgan operations)

sources.name: Official Records of the Union and Confederate Navies in the War of the Rebellion, Series I, Vol. 21
Chester Hearn, Mobile Bay and the Mobile Campaign (1993)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented through naval records and after-action reports. Farragut's exact wording of his famous order is debated by contemporaries.

events.title: Sand Creek Massacre
events.description: On 29 November 1864, approximately 700 Colorado Territory militia under Colonel John Chivington attacked a camp of Cheyenne and Arapaho people at Sand Creek, despite the camp being under the protection of the U.S. government and flying both an American flag and a white flag. Between 150 and 200 Native Americans were killed, the majority women, children, and elderly. The militia committed widespread mutilations and atrocities. The massacre provoked outrage even among contemporaries; congressional investigations in 1865 condemned Chivington's actions. Sand Creek intensified the Indian Wars on the Great Plains and permanently destroyed prospects for peaceful coexistence in the region.
key_figures: Black Kettle (Cheyenne chief, survived Sand Creek but was killed at Washita in 1868)

sources.name: Report of the Joint Committee on the Conduct of the War, 38th Congress (1865)
Testimony of John Smith, Indian interpreter, before the Joint Committee (1865)
Ari Kelman, A Misplaced Massacre: Struggling over the Memory of Sand Creek (2013)

events.certainty_note: Documented by multiple congressional investigations, military testimony, and survivor accounts from both sides. Casualty numbers are estimated based on conflicting accounts.

events.title: Maximilian arrives in Mexico as Emperor
events.description: On 28 May 1864, Archduke Maximilian of Austria arrived in Veracruz and assumed the title Emperor of Mexico, installed by French military intervention under Napoleon III. The Second Mexican Empire was a product of France's opportunistic intervention during the U.S. Civil War, when the Monroe Doctrine could not be enforced. Maximilian's rule was supported by French troops, Mexican conservatives, and the Church, but was opposed by the republican government of Benito Juárez, which continued guerrilla resistance. Maximilian's surprisingly liberal policies alienated his conservative base without winning over republicans, setting the stage for his eventual defeat and execution in 1867.
key_figures: Napoleon III (Emperor of France, sponsor of the intervention)
Benito Juárez (President of the Mexican Republic, in exile/resistance)
Marshal François Achille Bazaine (French military commander in Mexico)

sources.name: Maximilian's correspondence and decrees (various archives)
M.M. McAllen, Maximilian and Carlota: Europe's Last Empire in Mexico (2014)
Edward Shawcross, The Last Emperor of Mexico (2021)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented through diplomatic archives, press, and official records from Mexico, France, and Austria.

events.title: International Workingmen's Association (First International) founded
events.description: On 28 September 1864, the International Workingmen's Association (later known as the First International) was founded at a meeting at St. Martin's Hall in London. The organisation brought together trade unionists, socialists, anarchists, and republicans from across Europe. Karl Marx drafted its inaugural address and played a dominant role in shaping its direction. Though the organisation was internally fractious — particularly between Marxists and followers of Mikhail Bakunin — it represented the first significant attempt at international coordination of the labour movement and profoundly influenced the development of socialist and communist politics for decades.
key_figures: Karl Marx (drafted inaugural address, General Council member)
George Odger (English trade unionist, chair of founding meeting)

sources.name: Inaugural Address and Provisional Rules of the International Working Men's Association (1864, Marx's text)
Minutes of the founding meeting, St. Martin's Hall, 28 September 1864
G.M. Stekloff, History of the First International (1928)

events.certainty_note: The founding documents, Marx's correspondence about the event, and contemporary press reports survive.

events.title: Bhutan War — British annexation of Bhutanese duars
events.description: In 1864, hostilities between British India and Bhutan escalated into the Duar War (also Bhutan War) following the failed Ashley Eden mission to Bhutan earlier in the year. Eden, sent to negotiate over disputed frontier territories (the duars — lowland passes between Bhutan and Assam/Bengal), was humiliated by the Bhutanese court. Britain retaliated by annexing the Bengal Duars and launching military incursions. The war continued into 1865, ending with the Treaty of Sinchula, which ceded the duars to British India in exchange for an annual subsidy. This marked the beginning of Bhutan's semi-subordinate relationship with British India.

sources.name: Ashley Eden, Report on the State of Bootan and the Progress of the Mission (1864)
Karma Phuntsho, The History of Bhutan (2013)

events.certainty_note: Documented through British colonial records and Eden's mission report. Bhutanese sources for this period are limited.

events.title: Nian Rebellion and continued Qing pacification campaigns
events.description: While the Taiping Rebellion ended in 1864, the Qing dynasty continued to face the Nian Rebellion (active since 1851) across the North China Plain, particularly in Anhui, Shandong, Henan, and Jiangsu provinces. The Nian, organised into mobile cavalry-based forces, proved difficult to suppress through conventional means. After the Taiping collapse, the Qing redirected military resources — particularly the modernised Huai Army under Li Hongzhang — northward. The Nian Rebellion was not fully suppressed until 1868. Additionally, Muslim rebellions in Yunnan (Panthay Rebellion, 1856–1873) and the northwest (Dungan Revolt, 1862–1877) continued to challenge Qing authority.
key_figures: Sengge Rinchen (Mongol prince, Qing commander, killed by Nian in 1865)

sources.name: Qing dynastic records (Qing shilu)
Elizabeth Perry, Rebels and Revolutionaries in North China, 1845–1945 (1980)

events.certainty_note: Documented through Qing military records and Western observer accounts, though details of Nian organisation remain less well understood than the Taiping movement.

events.title: Bombardment of Shimonoseki — Western powers force Japan's hand
events.description: On 5–6 September 1864, a combined fleet of British, French, Dutch, and American warships bombarded the Chōshū domain's coastal fortifications at Shimonoseki in retaliation for the domain's attacks on Western shipping in 1863. The overwhelming firepower of the Western fleet demolished the fortifications and forced Chōshū to accept a ceasefire and indemnity. The defeat convinced Chōshū's leaders — key figures in the coming Meiji Restoration — that Japan must modernise its military along Western lines rather than resist through exclusion (jōi). This pragmatic reorientation was a crucial precondition for the overthrow of the Tokugawa shogunate in 1868.
key_figures: Itō Hirobumi (Chōshū activist, future Prime Minister of Japan)

sources.name: British Admiralty records and dispatches, 1864
Marius Jansen, Sakamoto Ryōma and the Meiji Restoration (1961)
W.G. Beasley, The Meiji Restoration (1972)

events.certainty_note: Documented by naval records from all participating nations and Japanese domain records.

events.title: Circassian genocide and mass expulsion reaches climax
events.description: In 1864, Russia completed its conquest of the western Caucasus, culminating in the mass expulsion and killing of the Circassian peoples. On 21 May 1864, Russian forces celebrated victory at Kbaada (now Krasnaya Polyana), marking the official end of the Russo-Circassian War. An estimated 500,000 to 1.5 million Circassians were expelled to the Ottoman Empire, with hundreds of thousands dying during the deportations from starvation, disease, and exposure during forced marches and sea crossings. The event is recognised by modern Circassian communities and some scholars as genocide, though this characterisation remains politically contested. The depopulation of the western Caucasus was near-total.
key_figures: Grand Duke Michael Nikolaevich (Viceroy of the Caucasus)

sources.name: Russian military dispatches and victory proclamations, May 1864
Ottoman resettlement records for Circassian refugees
Walter Richmond, The Circassian Genocide (2013)

events.certainty_note: The military conquest is thoroughly documented in Russian archives. Deportation scale is confirmed by Russian, Ottoman, and Western consular sources, though exact death tolls are estimated. Genocide classification is debated in scholarship.

events.title: War of the Triple Alliance begins (Paraguayan War)
events.description: In late 1864, tensions over influence in Uruguay escalated into a continental war. Brazilian forces invaded Uruguay in October in support of the Colorado faction, prompting Paraguayan President Francisco Solano López to seize the Brazilian province of Mato Grosso in December 1864. This triggered the War of the Triple Alliance (1864–1870), which pitted Paraguay against Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay. The war would become the deadliest conflict in South American history, killing an estimated 60–90% of Paraguay's male population and devastating the country for generations.
key_figures: Venancio Flores (Uruguayan Colorado leader, installed with Brazilian support)

sources.name: Diplomatic correspondence between Paraguay, Brazil, and Argentina, 1864
Thomas Whigham, The Paraguayan War, Volume I: Causes and Early Conduct (2002)
Luc Capdevila, Une guerre totale: Paraguay, 1864–1870 (2007)

events.certainty_note: Diplomatic and military records survive from all belligerents. Paraguayan demographic losses are estimated, as comprehensive pre-war census data is limited.

events.title: Confederate commerce raider CSS Alabama sunk
events.description: On 19 June 1864, the Union warship USS Kearsarge sank the Confederate commerce raider CSS Alabama off the coast of Cherbourg, France, in one of the most famous naval engagements of the Civil War. The Alabama, built in Britain and commanded by Captain Raphael Semmes, had captured or destroyed 65 Union merchant ships over two years, severely disrupting Northern commerce. The battle was watched by spectators from the Cherbourg shore. The Alabama's career and the diplomatic controversy over its British construction later resulted in the Alabama Claims, which established important precedents in international arbitration.

sources.name: Official Records of the Union and Confederate Navies in the War of the Rebellion, Series I, Vol. 3
Semmes, Memoirs of Service Afloat During the War Between the States (1869)
Chester Hearn, Gray Raiders of the Sea (1992)

events.certainty_note: Naval logs, official reports, and eyewitness accounts (including by artist Édouard Manet) thoroughly document the engagement.

events.title: Dungannon Convention and Fenian Brotherhood activity intensifies
events.description: In 1864, the Irish Republican Brotherhood (IRB) and its American counterpart, the Fenian Brotherhood, intensified organisational activities for an armed insurrection against British rule in Ireland. The Fenian Brotherhood held a major convention in Chicago in November 1864, with delegates debating strategy and fundraising. Irish-American Civil War veterans provided a growing pool of military-trained recruits. Meanwhile, the IRB expanded its network across Ireland and Britain. These preparations laid the groundwork for the Fenian Rising of 1867, though British intelligence infiltration would ultimately compromise the movement.
key_figures: John O'Mahony (Head of Fenian Brotherhood in America)
James Stephens (Head of IRB in Ireland)

sources.name: Fenian Brotherhood convention proceedings, Chicago, 1864
R.V. Comerford, The Fenians in Context: Irish Politics and Society, 1848–82 (1985)

events.certainty_note: Convention records, British intelligence files, and American press coverage document Fenian activities in this period.

events.title: Tswana peoples resist Boer expansion at the Battle of Dimawe
events.description: In 1864, Kwena (Tswana) forces under Kgosi Sechele I and allied Ngwaketse under Gaseitsiwe successfully resisted Boer expansion from the Transvaal into what is now Botswana. Conflicts over territory, cattle, and labour had been escalating through the 1850s–60s. While not a single decisive pitched battle, the sustained resistance of Tswana polities in this period was a key factor in preventing Boer absorption of the region and ultimately contributed to British declaration of the Bechuanaland Protectorate in 1885.

sources.name: London Missionary Society records and missionary correspondence
Neil Parsons, A New History of Southern Africa (1993)

events.certainty_note: Dating of specific engagements is approximate. The broader pattern of Tswana resistance to Boer expansion in the 1860s is well attested in missionary and colonial records, but indigenous accounts were not recorded contemporaneously.

events.title: Satsuma-Chōshū rapprochement begins in Japan
events.description: In the aftermath of the Shimonoseki bombardment and the failed Chōshū expedition by the shogunate (the Kinmon Incident of August 1864 and First Chōshū Expedition), the political landscape in Japan shifted. Chōshū, humiliated by both Western powers and the shogunate, began internal reforms under leaders like Takasugi Shinsaku. Meanwhile, Satsuma — which had clashed with Chōshū at the Kinmon Incident — began to recognise shared interests against the Tokugawa regime. These developments laid the groundwork for the secret Satsuma-Chōshū Alliance of 1866, brokered by Sakamoto Ryōma, which would be the decisive political realignment enabling the Meiji Restoration.

sources.name: Domain records of Chōshū and Satsuma (han archives)
W.G. Beasley, The Meiji Restoration (1972)
Mark Ravina, The Last Samurai: The Life and Battles of Saigō Takamori (2004)

events.certainty_note: Japanese domain records and the extensive scholarship on bakumatsu politics document the internal shifts, though the private calculations of domain leaders involve some reconstruction.

events.title: Nevada admitted as 36th U.S. state
events.description: On 31 October 1864, Nevada was admitted to the Union as the 36th state, just eight days before the presidential election. The rushed admission was politically motivated: Lincoln and the Republican Party wanted additional electoral votes and congressional support for the proposed Thirteenth Amendment abolishing slavery. Nevada's small population (under 40,000) would not normally have qualified it for statehood, but its silver mines made it economically important to the Union war effort, and its state constitution was telegraphed to Washington — the longest telegram transmission to that date — to meet the deadline.
key_figures: James Nye (first Governor of Nevada Territory)

sources.name: Congressional Record and presidential proclamation, 31 October 1864
Michael Green, Nevada: A History of the Silver State (2015)

events.certainty_note: State admission is a matter of official record. The political motivations are well documented in contemporary correspondence.

disconfirming_evidence: Several points of dispute or complication deserve note: (1) The death toll of the Taiping Rebellion (1864-001) is frequently cited as 20–30 million, but recent scholarship (Platt, 2012) notes that these figures are extrapolated from incomplete census data and may be significantly over- or understated; some estimates range as high as 70 million. (2) Admiral Farragut's famous quote 'Damn the torpedoes, full speed ahead!' (1864-010) was not recorded verbatim by any contemporary source; the earliest accounts vary in wording, and the phrase may have been polished for dramatic effect in post-war retellings. (3) The characterisation of Sand Creek (1864-011) as a 'battle' was the language used by Chivington and his defenders; contemporary congressional investigations and modern scholarship overwhelmingly classify it as a massacre, though Chivington was never formally punished. (4) Grant's alleged regret over Cold Harbor (1864-008) — 'I have always regretted that the last assault at Cold Harbor was ever made' — appears in his Memoirs but some historians note that Grant's memoirs were written while dying of cancer and edited by Mark Twain's publishing house, raising questions about how precisely the sentiment was his own versus shaped by editorial revision. (5) The Circassian expulsion (1864-017) is classified as genocide by Circassian diaspora communities and some scholars, but Russia officially rejects this characterisation, and the event remains far less recognised internationally than comparable nineteenth-century atrocities. (6) Casualty figures for the War of the Triple Alliance (1864-018), particularly the claim that 60–90% of Paraguay's male population died, have been challenged by revisionist historians (e.g., Thomas Whigham and Barbara Potthast) as likely inflated by early nationalist historiography, though the demographic catastrophe was undeniably severe.
historiographic_note: The year 1864 is richly documented from European, American, and East Asian sources, but the available record is heavily shaped by the perspectives of state actors, military commanders, and literate elites. The American Civil War dominates Anglophone historiography, often crowding out other significant global events. Chinese sources for the Taiping Rebellion's conclusion are primarily from the victorious Qing side; Taiping sources were largely destroyed. Southern African and Central Asian events are mediated through colonial and missionary records. The Circassian expulsion, despite its enormous scale, has received relatively little Western scholarly attention compared to contemporaneous events. Indigenous perspectives from the Americas (Sand Creek) and the Pacific are preserved primarily through later oral testimony and congressional investigation records rather than contemporary indigenous documentation.

graph_edges.note: End of the Taiping Rebellion enabled the Tongzhi Restoration and Self-Strengthening Movement
Overland Campaign's inconclusive battles led Grant to the Petersburg siege strategy
Siege of Petersburg ended with the fall of Petersburg and Richmond in April 1865
Fall of Atlanta revived Northern morale, ensuring Lincoln's re-election
Sherman's march continued northward through the Carolinas in early 1865
Joint administration of Schleswig-Holstein created the pretext for the 1866 war
First Geneva Convention was expanded and revised in subsequent conventions
Lincoln's re-election mandate enabled passage of the Thirteenth Amendment
Sand Creek escalated Plains Indian Wars; Black Kettle survived here only to be killed at Washita
Maximilian's Mexican Empire collapsed after French withdrawal; he was executed in 1867
First International provided organisational framework that influenced the Commune
Shimonoseki defeat convinced Chōshū to modernise, contributing to the Meiji Restoration
Circassian refugees in Ottoman territory became a factor in Russo-Turkish tensions
War of the Triple Alliance continued until Paraguay's near-total defeat in 1870
The Alabama's career prompted international arbitration claims against Britain
Internal reforms in Chōshū and pragmatic shifts in Satsuma led to their 1866 alliance
